content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
· 1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Gary Edwards 
Sonoma City Councilmember 
1 The Plaza 
Sonoma, CA 95476 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-18-136 
Dear Mr. Edwards: 
August 6, 2018 
This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict of interest provisions of 
the Political Reform Act (the "Act"). 1 Please note that we are only providing advice under the 
conflict of interest provisions of the Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions 
such as common law conflict of interest or Section 1090. Also note that we are not a finder of fact 
when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes 
your facts are complete and accurate. If this is not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions 
should change, you should contact us for additional advice. 
QUESTION 
Does the Act prohibit you, a Sonoma City Councilmember, from taking part in 
governmental decisions relating to a hotel development project (the "hotel project") located 590 feet 
from commercial rental property owned by your family trust? 
CONCLUSION 
No. Th~ Act does not prohibit you from taking part in those decisions because they would 
not have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on any of your financial interests based 
on the facts presented. 
FACTS AS PROVIDED BY REQUESTOR 
You are a Sonoma City Councilmember. You, your wife, and your children are trustees and 
lifetime beneficiaries of your family trust. Your family trust is the 100-percent owner of a 
commercial rental property located in the City, within one city block of the Sonoma Plaza. The trust 
1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


File No. A-18-136 
Page No. 2 
leases the commercial rental property to a privately-held mortgage broker business. You note that 
your interest in that property has a value more than $2,000. 
The Sonoma Plaza, a National Historic Landmark, serves as a focal point within the City, 
hosting many community festivals and drawing tourists throughout the year. The area surrounding 
the Plaza is characterized by thriving businesses which cater to community members and tourists, 
including at least five existing hotels within 400 feet of the Plaza, all of which are also within 1,050 
feet of your trust's commercial rental property. 
The City will soon consider whether to approve the hotel project at a site located kitty 
comer from the southwest comer of the Sonoma Plaza. The project site is made up of several 
parcels, with a total area of 1.24 acres. The site is approximately 140 feet from the Plaza, and 
approximately 590 feet from your trust's commercial rental property as the crow flies. Directly 
southeast of the site there is an existing 82-room hotel, which is within 400 feet of the Plaza. 
The hotel project envisions a 62-room hotel and spa, with an 80-seat restaurant and site 
improvements. You state that an environmental analysis was prepared for the proposed hotel 
project, indicating the project will not generate any impacts on the commercial rental property, and 
that the traffic generated by the project will not affect traffic at the intersection of East Napa Street 
and First Street East, the intersection closest to the commercial rental property. You also note that 
the project's proposed structures will not be visible from the commercial rental property. 
To mitigate parking impacts, the project would include a new 94-space underground parking 
lot and retain existing on-street parking. The City indicates that further parking mitigation measures 
would be available should a parking shortfall occur despite the addition of the underground lot. 
ANALYSIS 
The Act's conflict of interest provisions prohibit a public official from making, participating 
in making, or using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision if that decision 
will have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on one or more of the official's financial 
interests distinguishable from the effect on the public generally. (Sections 87100 and 87103.) An 
official's financial interests in a decision which may give rise to a disqualifying effect are set forth 
in Section 87103 and include the following: 
• 
Any business entity in which the official has an investment worth $2,000 or more (subdivision 
(a)), or in which the official is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds any 
position of management (subdivision (d)). 
• 
Any real property in which the official has ah interest worth $2,000 or more. (Subdivision (b).) 
• 
Any source of income aggregating $500 or more in value to the official within 12 months prior 
to the decision. (Subdivision (c).) 
• 
Any donor of a gift or gifts aggregating $470 or more in value to the official within 12 months 
prior to the decision. (Subdivision ( e ). ) 


File No. A-18-136 
Page No. 3 
• 
An official always has an interest in his or her personal finances or those of immediate family 
members. 
The Act defines the term "business entity" as "any organization or enterprise operated for 
profit, including but not limited to a proprietorship, firm, business, trust, joint venture, syndicate, 
corporation or association." (Section 82005.) Although an official may have a business interest in a 
business trust, an official does not typically have a business interest in an ordinary family trust. (See 
Hodge Advice Letter, No. A-13-002 and Nebb Advice Letter, No. I-08-168.) The Nebb Advice 
Letter, supra, explained the distinction as follows: 
The principal distinction between a business trust and an ordinary trust is 
found in the purpose. The purpose of a business trust is to carry on a business 
enterprise for profit whereas the purpose of the ordinary trust is to conserve 
trust property with incidental powers of sale and investment. 
Your trust runs a property management business which appears to manage the commercial 
rental property at issue for profit. Therefore, based on the facts presented, you have a business 
interest in your family trust. 
Regulation 18234(a) provides that an official has an interest in the pro rata share of a trust's 
real property, sources of income, and investments when the official's direct, indirect, or beneficial 
interest in the trust is 10 percent or greater. Under Regulation l 8234(b ), an official's interest in the 
real property, sources of income, and investments of a trust include those of the official, spouse, and 
dependent children regarding the trust's real property and investments and those of the official and 
spouse regarding sources of income. 
You state that your trust is 100-percent owner of the commercial rental property at issue, 
and that the trustees and lifetime beneficiaries of the trust are you, your wife, and your children. 
Therefore, you have an interest in the trust's real property, sources of income, and investments 
because you have an interest of 10 percent or greater in the trust pursuant to Regulation 18234(a). 
Thus, with respect to the decisions at issue, you have the following interests: a business 
interest in your family trust; a real property interest in the trust's commercial rental property; source 
of income interests in the trust, the trust's property management business, and in the mortgage-
broker tenant of the commercial rental property, assuming you receive income of $500 or more 
from each source in the 12 months prior to the decisions at issue; and an interest in your personal 
finances. 2 
Foreseeability and Materiality 
Regulation 18701 sets forth the Act's standards for whether a decision's effect on an 
official's interest is reasonably foreseeable. With respect to an official's interest that is not 
2 An effect on an official's interest in his or her personal finances is not considered separately if the decision 
also affects the official's interest in a business entity or real property (Regulation 18702.S(c)). Therefore, we do not 
further analyze your interest in your personal finances because the decisions relating to the hotel project also affect your 
business interest in the trust and your real property interest in the commercial rental property. 


File No. A-18-136 
Page No. 4 
explicitly involved in a decision,3 Regulation 18701(b) provides that the decision's effect is 
reasonably foreseeable if it "can be recognized as a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical 
or theoretical." 
Business and Source of Income Interests 
Regulation 18702.3 sets forth the materiality standards applicable to a decision's effect on 
an official's interest in a source of income. Regulation 18702.3(a)(3) provides that a decision's 
effect on an official's interest in a source of income that is a business is material if that effect would 
be material under the materiality standards applicable to a business set forth in Regulation 18702.1. 
Because your sources of income are businesses, we apply Regulation 18702.1 to the effect of the 
decisions relating to the hotel project on those businesses. 
Regulation 18702.1 sets forth the materiality standards applicable to a decision's effect on 
an official's interest in a business entity. Regulation 18702.1 (b) provides that the decision's effect is 
material if a prudent person with sufficient information would find it reasonably foreseeable that the 
effect would contribute to.a change in the value of a privately held business entity. That provision 
also enumerates examples of types of decisions that may have a material effect, including decisions 
that would"[ d]ecide the location of a major development, entertainment facility, or other project 
that would increase or decrease the amount of business the business entity draws from the location 
of the project." (Regulation 18702.2(b)(5).) 
While the hotel project would result in the construction of a 62-room hotel and spa and an 
80-seat restaurant if approved, there are already many thriving businesses that cater to community 
members and tourists near your trust's commercial rental property, including at least five existing 
hotels within 1,050 feet of that property. Although the decisions relating to the hotel project would 
determine the location of the project, there is no indication that development of the project at the 
proposed site would result in an increase or decrease in the amount of business that the trust, 
property management business, or mortgage-broker tenant derives from that location, as these 
businesses are not dependent on increased foot or vehicle traffic for additional business. Therefore, 
a prudent person with sufficient information would not find it reasonably foreseeable that the 
decisions relating to the project would contribute to a change in value of the trust, property 
management business, or mortgage-broker tenant. 
Real Property Interest in the Trust's Commercial Rental Property 
Regulation 18702.2 provides the materiality standards applicable to a decision's effect on an 
official's real property interest. Of those standards, the standards set forth in Regulation 
18702.2(a)(10) and Regulation 18702.2(a)(12) are applicable to determine whether the decisions 
relating to the hotel project would have a disqualifying effect on your real property interest in the 
trust's commercial rental property at issue. Under those materiality standards, a decision's effect on 
an official's real property interest is material if it: 
3 An official's interest is "explicitly involve.ct" in a decision if it is "a named party in, or the subject of," the 
decision. (Regulation 1870I(a).) Because none of your interests is a named party in or the subject of the decisions 
relating to the hotel project, those interests are not explicitly involved in those decisions. 


File No. A-18-136 
Page No. 5 
(10) Would change the character of the parcel of real property by substantially 
altering traffic levels or intensity of use, including parking, of property 
surrounding the official's real property parcel, the view, privacy, noise levels, 
or air quality, including odors, or any other factors that would affect the market 
value of the real property parcel in which the official has a financial interest; 
(12) Would cause a reasonably prudent person, using due care and 
consideration under the circumstances, to believe that the governmental 
decision was of such a nature that its reasonably foreseeable effect would 
influence the market value of the official's property. 
Your trust's commercial rental property is within 590 feet of the Sonoma Plaza, within an 
area of the City already characterized by thriving businesses that cater to community members and 
tourists. You state that an environmental analysis prepared for the proposed hotel project indicates 
that the project will not generate any impacts on the commercial rental property, and that the traffic 
generated by the project would not affect traffic at the intersection of East Napa Street and First 
Street East, the intersection closest to the commercial rental property. To mitigate parking impacts 
from the project, the project would include a new 94-space underground parking lot, existing on-
street parking would be maintained, and staff has identified additional parking mitigation measures 
in case of any potential parking shortfall. You also note that the project's proposed structures will 
not be visible from the commercial rental property. While the hotel project may lead to some 
adverse impacts on the commercial rental property during the project's construction, those impacts 
would be temporary, and would not affect the value of that property. Therefore, decisions relating 
to the project would not affect or influence the market value of the commercial rental property. 
Thus, we conclude that the Act does not prohibit you from taking part in governmental 
decisions relating to the hotel project because those decisions would not have a reasonably 
foreseeable material financial effect on any of your financial interests based on the facts presented. 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
MFC:jgl 
Sincerely, 
Brian G. Lau 
Acting General Counsel 
By: 
Matthew F. Christy 
Counsel, Legal Division

in making, or using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision if that decision

(a)), or in which the official is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds any 
position of management (subdivision (d)). 
• 
Any real property in which the official has ah interest worth $2,000 or more. (Subdivision (b).) 
• 
Any source of income aggregating $500 or more in value to the official within 12 months prior 
to the decision. (Subdivision (c).) 
• 
Any donor of a gift or gifts aggregating $470 or more in value to the official within 12 months 
prior to the decision. (Subdivision ( e ). )  
• 
An official always has an interest in his or her personal finances or those of immediate family 
members. 
The Act defines the term "business entity" as "any organization or enterprise operated for

from each source in the 12 months prior to the decisions at issue; and an interest in your personal 
finances. 2

Regulation 18701 sets forth the Act's standards for whether a decision's effect on an 
official's interest is reasonably foreseeable. With respect to an official's interest that is not 
2 An effect on an official's interest in his or her personal finances is not considered separately if the decision 
also affects the official's interest in a business entity or real property (Regulation 18702.S(c)). Therefore, we do not 
further analyze your interest in your personal finances because the decisions relating to the hotel project also affect your 
business interest in the trust and your real property interest in the commercial rental property.  
explicitly involved in a decision,3 Regulation 18701(b) provides that the decision's effect is 
reasonably foreseeable if it "can be recognized as a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical

Your trust's commercial rental property is within 590 feet of the Sonoma Plaza, within an 
area of the City already characterized by thriving businesses that cater to community members and 
tourists. You state that an environmental analysis prepared for the proposed hotel project indicates 
that the project will not generate any impacts on the commercial rental property, and that the traffic 
generated by the project would not affect traffic at the intersection of East Napa Street and First 
Street East, the intersection closest to the commercial rental property. To mitigate parking impacts 
from the project, the project would include a new 94-space underground parking lot, existing on-

governmental decisions relating to a hotel development project (the "hotel project") located 590 feet 
from commercial rental property owned by your family trust?

CONCLUSION: No. Th~ Act does not prohibit you from taking part in those decisions because they would 
not have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on any of your financial interests based 
on the facts presented.
